1.description: Local currency debt shares and bond betas. Panel A shows the LC bond-local stock beta versus the share of LC debt as a fraction of total central government in percentage over the period 2005 to 2014. Panel B shows the LC bond-stock beta versus the share of LC debt in U.S. investors' holdings of government debt from the TIC data over the period 2007 to 2014. For each country, the LC bond-stock beta is estimated as the slope coefficient of quarterly LC bond log excess returns onto local stock market log excess returns over the same period,xri,n,tLC=ai+β(bondi,stocki)×xri,tm+εi,t. The solid diamond denotes emerging markets and the hollow circle denotes developed countries. Three-letter codes indicate currencies. For a list of currency codes, see Internet Appendix Section I.A.

2.description: Local currency debt shares and LC minus FC bond-stock beta. Panels A and B show the LC minus FC bond-stock beta,β(bondiLC−FC,stocki), versus the LC bond-stock beta,β(bondi,stocki). Panels C and D show the LC minus FC bond-stock beta versus the LC debt share of percentage. In Panels A and C, the FC bond yield is estimated using the sum of the U.S. Treasury yield and the sovereign CDS spread. In Panels B and D, the FC bond yield is estimated using the difference between the LC bond yield and the cross-currency swap rate between the LC and the dollar. More details on the construction of the LC and FC bond-stock beta differential can be found in SectionI.F. The solid diamond denotes emerging markets and the hollow circle denotes developed countries. Three-letter codes indicate currencies. For a list of currency codes, see Internet Appendix Section I.A.

3.description: LC Bond-U.S. Stock Beta and LC Bond Risk Premia. This figure plots the average risk premium on LC bonds against the LC bond-U.S. stock beta. LC bond risk premia are estimated according to equation (10). The LC bond-U.S. stock beta is estimated from the regressionxri,n,tLC=ai+β(bondi,stockUS)×xrUS,tm+εi,t. The solid diamond denotes emerging markets and the hollow circle denotes developed countries. Three-letter codes indicate currencies. For a list of currency codes, see Appendix Section I.A.

4.description: Model timeline. This figure shows the timing of the government's problem without commitment and with commitment.

5.description: Optimal government policy versus international lender risk aversion. This figure shows the model LC bond-stock beta and the LC debt share for the EM and DM calibrations. The solid blue lines indicate the EM calibration, while the dashed red lines indicate the DM calibration. Both panels vary international lenders' coefficient of risk aversion,γ∗, along thex-axis while holding all other parameters constant at the values shown in TableV. A vertical line indicatesγ∗=29.74(i.e., equal lender and domestic risk aversion). Because LC bond returns in the model are negatively related with inflation and stock returns are modeled as proportional to output news, a government that optimally chooses a countercyclical optimal inflation policy will show up with a positive LC bond-stock beta. For details on the relationship between model LC bond-stock beta and inflation-output beta, see Internet Appendix Section II.B. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
